finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the SIU and may be briefly summarized. In the evening of early 2026, the affected person was with her boyfriend, an off-duty officer, on a street in Toronto when the two quarreled and she left the area telling him that she was going to jump. She made her way to a bridge over a river and called the officer using a passerby’s cellphone. She told him she was going to commit suicide and that it would be his fault. The officer called police to report that the affected person was in mental health crisis and threatening to harm herself. He would subsequently learn from a third-party whose phone the affected person had used to call him that she was in the area of the bridge and a roadway. The subject officer drove in that direction, travelling across the bridge. With him was another officer. The subject officer approached the side of the bridge, brought his vehicle to a stop and exited with the other officer. They were just out of their cruiser when the affected person scaled the bridge guardrail and jumped down the other side. She landed on the frozen river below, a distance of about eight metres. The two officers made their way to the riverbank and walked onto the river to render aid to the affected person. Firefighters attended the scene and assisted in her removal. She was transported to hospital and diagnosed with serious injuries.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: In the morning of early 2026, police officers were on the lookout in a regional area for a SUV that had reportedly been involved in several collisions and was said to be operated in a reckless manner - speeding, swerving and skidding on the roadway. It had sustained significant damage to the front passenger side. An officer was among the officers responding to one of the collision scenes, travelling in one direction, when he spotted the SUV passing him in the opposite direction. The officer performed a U-turn and accelerated after the vehicle, activating his cruiser’s emergency lights and siren. A short time later, the SUV pulled over to the shoulder of the road and came to a stop. The officer stopped a short distance behind the SUV. The driver was alone in the vehicle. He opened his driver’s door, observed the officer approaching him on foot, and asked the officer what he had done. The officer said he was driving a stolen vehicle. The driver said the vehicle was not stolen. Told he was being detained for a “hit and run”, the driver asked for more information. The driver refused to exit the SUV at the officer’s direction and then began to grapple with him on the road when the officer dragged him out. Another officer was responding to the first officer’s radio request for backup when he arrived on scene to find the two fighting in the middle of the road. The officer exited his cruiser and ran to the altercation just as the first officer was bringing the driver down against the SUV. As the two continued to wrestle each other, the second officer threw several punches in the direction of the driver’s head. Following the final blow, the driver brought his hands to cover his face. He was turned onto his front and handcuffed behind the back. Following his arrest, the driver was seen at hospital and diagnosed with a fractured right orbital bone.

finding: substantiated
summary_redacted_fr: La Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire a tenu une audience d'intérêt public portant sur des plaintes déposées par la famille d'un membre des Forces canadiennes décédé par suicide. Les enquêtes menées par le Service national des enquêtes des Forces canadiennes en 2008, 2009 et 2010 ont été examinées, notamment la gestion de la scène de décès, la non-divulgation de la note de suicide à la famille, les lacunes professionnelles et d'indépendance des enquêteurs, ainsi que les interactions avec les plaignants. La Commission a formulé de nombreuses conclusions et recommandations à l'intention du Grand Prévôt des Forces canadiennes.
tribunal_body: Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire (CPPM) — dossier CPPM 2011-004

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police witnesses and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the early morning of early 2026, an officer and the subject officer were dispatched to an area in Toronto, following a 911 call. The caller did not address the call-taker but utterances were heard in the background suggesting an ongoing and potentially violent disturbance involving a male and female. The officers entered the home despite objections from the woman who opened the door – the affected person. The affected person was placed under arrest by an officer after they observed a male – the affected person – with a cut to the forehead. The affected person refused to cooperate as the officer sought to question them about what had happened. Standing on a second floor landing, the affected person asked the officers to leave and then called 911 asking for additional officers. The officers told the affected person to disconnect the 911 call as they were the police. When they failed to do so, they warned that they would be arrested for public mischief. Shortly after, the officers took hold of the affected person to take them into custody. The affected person refused to surrender their arms to be handcuffed and was grounded. On the floor, they resisted the officers’ efforts to control their arms behind the back and was met with a punch to the face by the subject officer. Moments later, as the struggle continued, the subject officer delivered two punches to the affected person’s head in quick succession. It would take another five minutes, during which an officer kicked the affected person once or twice in the legs and exhorted them verbally to give up their arms, before the officers wrestled control of their arms behind the back and secured them in handcuffs. The affected person was transported to hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with a broken nose.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and additional police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In early 2026, police officers were dispatched to a location in response to a report of a person attempting to break into a residence. Two witness officers were the first on scene, arriving at about 3:10 a.m. They were joined shortly by a police dog handler, the subject officer, and their dog. Additional officers arrived on scene and set up a perimeter around the house as the subject officer deployed their dog to locate the suspect. It was not long before the dog led the subject officer to a crawl space underneath a deck. The affected person was hiding in the crawl space, which was cluttered with stored items. They failed to come out and give any indication of their presence despite repeated direction from the subject officer for them to do so. With the dog still attached to the lead, the subject officer released the dog into the crawl space. The dog’s first foray into the space was unsuccessful. The subject officer released the dog into the crawl space again. On this occasion, the dog located the affected person under some items and bit into their left arm. The affected person gave no reaction. The subject officer approached their dog and the affected person and released the dog’s hold, after which the affected person was taken into custody. The affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a fracture and dog bites to the left arm.

finding: Rapport remis au DPCP

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and both subject officers, gives rise to the following scenario. In the afternoon of early 2026, members of a division's MCU were on the lookout for the affected person. They had information to believe that they had purchased a cellphone the day before with counterfeit money, and planned to arrest them for the offence. Operating unmarked vehicles and dressed in plainclothes, the officers located the affected person exiting an apartment building and tracked them to a grocery store, just around the corner from their residence in the area of a major street. They took up positions on the street outside the grocery store intending to take the affected person into custody on their exit from the store. Unaware they were being followed, the affected person left the store with a box of diapers in hand and started walking in the direction of their residence. They were confronted by a subject officer. The officer physically engaged the affected person and the two went to the ground by a stand of bicycle racks. Other members of the MCU arrived quickly at the site of the arrest, including another subject officer, and intervened. The subject officer kneed the affected person twice in the left torso. The affected person was handcuffed behind the back and transported to the police station. The affected person complained of pain in the left arm and was transported to hospital where X-rays proved inconclusive because of swelling in the area. They were told to return to the fracture clinic for a follow-up assessment. Later, the affected person was X-rayed again in hospital and diagnosed with a radial head fracture of the left elbow.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the evening of early 2026, police were dispatched to an apartment in the area of a city. The person who called 911 reported a disturbance involving the affected person. They wanted the affected person removed from their residence. The subject officer arrived on scene with another officer shortly before 11:00 p.m., joined shortly by a third officer. The person who called 911 met them outside the building and provided them a key to the apartment. One of the officers opened the door, stepped inside and was splashed with cooking oil from a bottle by the affected person. The affected person retreated into the kitchen area and was physically engaged by the officers. They were taken to the floor and handcuffed behind the back by the subject officer. The affected person was placed in the backseat of the subject officer's cruiser and transported to a police detention facility. En route, they slipped the handcuffs under their legs so that they were then positioned in front of their body. In statements made to police personnel at the station, they would explain that the pain in their left wrist was the result of this maneuver. The next day, the affected person was seen at a hospital and diagnosed with a fractured left wrist.

A person died from a massive hemorrhage caused by a cervical wound inflicted by a sharp or cutting object in a city, in a context of domestic violence involving a person in a state of toxic psychosis associated with significant substance use. The circumstances surrounding the death raise issues concerning the evaluation of dangerousness in the context of toxic psychosis, the sharing of information between interveners, and the application of the precautionary principle in emergency interventions.

An adolescent died by asphyxiation in a community. The coroner's report highlights concerns about the detection of psychological distress in young people and the effectiveness of support mechanisms in schools.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The material events in question are clear on the evidence collected by the agency and may briefly be summarized. In early 2025, police were called to a residence in a city in Ontario. A resident of the building had called police to report a disturbance coming from another unit. Two officers attended at the scene and knocked at the door. The disturbance involved the affected person and another individual. The affected person was subject to an order at the time preventing contact and communication with the other individual. Realizing police were outside the door and that they would be arrested for breaching the order, the affected person attempted to escape apprehension. They climbed through a window of their unit onto the roof of an adjoining structure, ran across the roof to an exterior staircase and started to descend. Two other officers had also responded to the disturbance call and were outside as the first two officers entered. They observed the affected person running on the roof and were waiting for them at the bottom of the staircase. When the affected person jumped over the staircase railing at the midway landing and collapsed on the ground below, the officers approached to take them into custody. The affected person complained of pain to their foot and was transported to hospital where they were diagnosed with a broken ankle.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted:
The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. The subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes, as was their legal right.

In the early afternoon of Q4 2025, Toronto Police Service (TPS) officers were called to a hotel located in the Yonge Street area of Toronto. Hotel staff had contacted police to seek the removal of a guest — the complainant. The complainant had caused a commotion in the lobby and accused hotel employees of being associated with persons seeking her death. The complainant was of unsound mind at the time of her interaction with the officers. Since checking into the hotel a few days earlier, the complainant had frequently attended the front desk to complain about persons wanting her dead. She had returned to her room by the time of the officers' arrival.

A group of TPS officers arrived on scene and, accompanied by the hotel manager, took the elevator to the 18th floor and attended at the complainant's room. Among them was the subject officer. The subject officer knocked on the door and asked for the complainant. The complainant indicated she had not called police and refused to open the door. The subject officer indicated that the officers would open the door if she did not let them in. The complainant did not respond. A female officer interjected and attempted to speak with the complainant, assuring her she was not in trouble and that they simply wanted to talk. Still, there was no response. Officers attempted to open the door, including with a master card key, but were unsuccessful as it had been double-locked from the inside. A TPS Mobile Crisis Intervention Team (MCIT) arrived on scene and also tried to have the complainant open the door.

On learning from hotel staff that the complainant's room had a Juliet balcony from which the complainant could jump, a witness officer entered an adjacent room and onto its balcony. From that vantage point, the officer observed that the door to the Juliet balcony was open. Looking down, the officer observed the complainant lying on the roof of the third floor and immediately radioed for paramedics. The roof was accessed by Toronto Fire Service. The complainant was pronounced deceased shortly thereafter.

At autopsy, the pathologist was of the preliminary view that the complainant's death was attributable to multiple blunt force trauma.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officers chose not to interview with the SIU or authorize the release of their notes. In early 2026, the subject officers, in the company of two other officers, attended at an apartment in Oshawa. A neighbouring tenant had called 911 to report the sounds of a woman screaming coming from the apartment. Officers knocked on the door and asked the woman to open it. She tried but was unable to open the door. She could be heard moaning in distress and saying that she had been choked. One of the officers retrieved a ram and the subject officer used it to break open the door. The woman was by the doorway and pointed in the direction of a hallway. The affected person appeared in the hallway. He was at the apartment in violation of a bail order. One of the officers ordered the affected person to put his hands behind his back. The affected person refused to do so and instead walked towards the officer. The officer grabbed a hold of the affected person and attempted to wrestle control of his arms behind his back. The affected person refused to release his arms. There followed a struggle during which the affected person was forced to the hallway floor and subjected to multiple punches by the officers, and the use of a CEW, before he was handcuffed behind his back. The affected person was taken into custody and transported to hospital on two occasions before he was eventually diagnosed with a broken rib.

summary_redacted_fr: La Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire a mené une enquête d'intérêt public à la suite d'une plainte alléguant que des policiers militaires avaient maltraité des détenus à l'étranger lors d'un exercice d'entraînement, et que le commandant sur place avait ignoré les accusations qui lui avaient été soumises. Après examen de documents et des entrevues avec des témoins, la Commission a conclu que les allégations n'étaient pas vérifiées et qu'aucune preuve de mauvais traitement des détenus n'avait été découverte. La Commission a néanmoins formulé des recommandations visant à améliorer les pratiques d'enquête de la police militaire, notamment en matière de prise de notes, de préparation des entrevues et de gestion des dossiers après une opération à l'étranger.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person, the subject officer and other police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. In the afternoon of early 2026, the subject officer and another officer were dispatched to a residence in rural Lambton County. A person had called police to request a welfare check, and the caller was aware that the individuals involved were prohibited from being in each other’s company. The officers arrived at the location and entered the apartment. One person was arrested for assault. The affected person was located in a bedroom and arrested by the subject officer for breach of a non-association condition. The subject officer handcuffed the affected person behind the back and escorted them down an external staircase. As the pair neared the bottom of the stairs, the subject officer stepped on the affected person’s left foot. The affected person was not wearing shoes or socks at the time. The affected person was seen at hospital after their arrest and diagnosed with a fractured left foot.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the subject officer and another police eyewitness, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. In the evening of early 2025, police officers, including the subject officer, were dispatched to a location in a town. A resident of the multi-unit location had contacted police to report concern with the wellbeing of a male – the affected person – causing a disturbance at the property. He had been talking to himself and threatening to kill someone. The subject officer arrived on scene, joined by two other officers. The officers attended at a unit of the building and arrested the affected person on a warrant in effect for their arrest for breach of a probation order. They were handcuffed behind the back, escorted outside and searched by the officers. A number of items were seized. The affected person was subsequently placed in the backseat of a cruiser and read their rights by an officer. The officer exited the cruiser, leaving the affected person alone. They and another officer returned to the unit to search the residence for firearms. Left alone in the cruiser, the affected person slipped their handcuffed arms below their legs and repositioned them to the front. They subsequently reached towards the crotch area of their pants, retrieved a bag and ingested a substance it contained. Shortly after the affected person’s consumption of the substance, the subject officer began to monitor them from outside the cruiser. The affected person brought their hands up to their mouth area on several occasions. Having concluded the search of the unit, an officer returned to the cruiser and checked on the affected person in the rear. The affected person was shaking and pale. Noting that the handcuffs were now to the front, and suspecting a drug overdose, the officer requested EMS. The affected person was removed from the cruiser, administered two doses of Narcan and placed in the recovery position pending the arrival of paramedics. EMS arrived and the affected person was taken to hospital and treated for drug overdose.

summary_redacted: In the early morning of February 2026, the subject officer sat in their marked cruiser in a parking lot at the southeast corner of a major intersection. They and their passenger were manning a perimeter point at that location. At about 2:43 a.m., two vehicles travelled past their location at excessive speed. The subject officer pulled onto the roadway and accelerated after the vehicles, intending to stop them in connection with their speed. With the driver of one vehicle were several passengers, including the affected person in the backseat. The vehicles continued at speeds as high as 170 km/h. Approaching a major road, the driver came upon traffic stopped at a red light, applied their brakes but crashed into two vehicles. The affected person suffered a fractured hip in the collision. The subject officer arrived on scene and arrested the driver for dangerous driving.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police witnesses, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the evening of early 2026, police officers were looking for the affected person. They had left the scene of a domestic disturbance threatening suicide and the police were concerned for their wellbeing. A “ping” of the cell phone in the affected person’s possession returned a location in a certain area. The subject officer, in the company of two witness officers, arrived in the area. There was no answer at the first house they approached but one of the witness officers had noticed a light on at the house next door. The affected person had fled the scene of the disturbance and taken refuge in an empty house in the area. When one of the witness officers initially approached the house and asked them to identify themselves, the affected person provided a false name. With the officer now off the property, the affected person made their way outside to the rear of the property and inside a steel shed. They had a knife in their possession. Not long after the witness officer had left the residence where they encountered the affected person, the officer learned that, in fact, they had been dealing with the affected person. They, the subject officer and the other witness officer returned to the residence and eventually located the affected person in the rear shed. The affected person displayed a knife and began to walk with purpose towards the officers, repeatedly telling them to shoot them. The subject officer and one of the witness officers walked away from the shed and ordered the affected person to drop the knife. They did so, throwing it away, and continued their advance. When they were within striking range of the subject officer, the officer punched them in the face. The affected person was felled by the blow but continued to resist on the ground by refusing to surrender their arms. The witness officers delivered several more strikes to the torso and legs, after which the affected person was handcuffed behind the back. The affected person was diagnosed at hospital after their arrest with a fractured left orbital bone.

summary_redacted: The Commission's Final Report regarding a shooting death in Buick Creek, B.C. was released. The incident involved a person who shot out the tires of a vehicle containing their family members, who were on their way to attend a wedding in another town. The driver of the vehicle was able to proceed to a nearby residence, but the person followed them and shot out the remaining tires. The family travelled on to the wedding and remained out of town until the matter could be resolved, on the advice of the police. Attempts were made by police to speak with the person, but they were not cooperative. The police Emergency Response Team was ultimately activated. The incident was investigated under the Royal Canadian Mounted Police Act, Subsection 45.72(2).
tribunal_body: CRCC file PC-2009-2863 — Commission's Final Report after Commissioner's Response Regarding the Shooting Death of Valeri George in Buick Creek, B.C.

summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person, a subject officer and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, a subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the evening of late 2025, police officers, including a subject officer, attended an apartment in the area of a city. The resident had contacted police to report that an inebriated person was in her apartment and she wanted them removed. The officers entered the apartment and approached the person in the living room. They were seated on a sofa and speaking on the phone with a police call-taker. After a period, the person ended their conversation on the phone, stood up from the sofa and walked a short distance towards the door. Just before the door, the officers took hold of their arms. The person attempted to free their arms and the officers maintained their grip. Other officers intervened and the person fell forward to the floor, striking their head off the edge of a wall in the process. Officers fell with the person and landed on top of them. They were handcuffed in quick order and taken into custody. Following their arrest, the person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a reduced level of consciousness and a scalp laceration with emphysema and subgaleal hematoma.

The Independent Investigations Office (IIO) is responsible for conducting investigations into all officer-related incidents which result in death or "serious harm" within the province of British Columbia. As the Chief Civilian Director of the IIO, they are required to review all investigations upon their conclusion, in order to determine whether they consider that an officer may have committed an offence under any enactment. If they conclude that an officer may have committed an offence, they are required to report the matter to Crown Counsel. If they do not make a report to Crown Counsel, they are permitted to publicly report the reasoning underlying their decision. This is a public report related to the investigation into the injury of an adult male that occurred in the city of Vancouver. The affected person sustained serious injuries after being shot in the hand with a rubber bullet by a member of the Emergency Response Team of the police service.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police witnesses, and video footage that largely captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer chose not to interview with the SIU or authorize the release of their notes. In the early morning of 2026, the affected person was driving in Brampton when a police cruiser started to follow their vehicle, attempting to pull them over. The affected person did not stop for the cruiser. They turned into some side streets, ultimately losing control of their vehicle because of icy road conditions and striking parked cars. The affected person exited their car and fled the scene on foot through some backyards. An officer was on patrol when their ALPR registered a hit on the affected person's vehicle, indicating that the registered owner was a suspended driver. The officer briefly chased the affected person on foot and broadcast that they were subject to arrest for failing to remain at the scene of an accident. The affected person made their way home to the area, about six kilometres east of the collision scene. The subject officer was waiting for them in the area and grabbed a hold of the affected person, throwing them to the ground, just as they were attempting to enter the house. The officer kept the affected person pinned to the ground with their body weight for a period before they were joined by another officer, responding to the scene. The affected person struggled against the officers' efforts to control their arms and bit an officer's finger. The subject officer kneed the affected person in the left ribs and pulled them onto their left side. The parties continued to wrestle for control of the affected person's arms and the subject officer delivered another knee strike to the torso. With the arrival and intervention of additional officers, the affected person was handcuffed to the back and taken into custody. The affected person was charged with multiple offences and released at the scene. They subsequently attended hospital and were diagnosed with a fractured left rib.

L'employé n'aurait pas participé à l'exercice et n'aurait pas consenti à l'intervention; il aurait subi un traitement agressif incluant l'usage excessif de la force, des propos violents, et une fouille de ses effets personnels. La présidente de la Commission des plaintes contre la police militaire a décidé de tenir une audience d'intérêt public, invoquant la gravité des allégations, les enjeux systémiques possibles liés au profilage racial, et l'intérêt public.

summary_redacted: The Independent Investigations Office (IIO) is responsible for conducting investigations into all officer-related incidents which result in death or "serious harm" within the province of British Columbia. As the Chief Civilian Director of the IIO, they are required to review all investigations upon their conclusion, in order to determine whether they consider that an officer may have committed an offence under any enactment. If they conclude that an officer may have committed an offence, they are required to report the matter to Crown Counsel. If they do not make a report to Crown Counsel, they are permitted to publicly report the reasoning underlying their decision. This is a public report related to the investigation into the injury of an affected person that occurred in the city of West Vancouver. The affected person sustained a serious injury, allegedly while being transported by officers. Pursuant to the Police Act, the IIO conducted an investigation and has made a decision regarding the matter.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person, the subject officer and additional police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. In early 2026, a team of officers with a regional police service, operating in plainclothes and unmarked vehicles with a specialized unit, were looking to arrest a group of males suspected in a series of crimes. One of them - the affected person – was also subject to an arrest warrant. The team – including multiple witness officers and the subject officer – tracked the suspects to a location in a city, the address of a public venue. The officers surveilled the vehicles and the males. The affected person removed items from the trunk of their vehicle and placed them on the bed of another vehicle. After a time, the officers decided to move in to arrest the suspects. The venue was closed, the parking lot was empty and most of the males were out of the vehicles. The plan was to approach the vehicles and box them in from the front and sides to prevent the males using them to escape. On seeing the unmarked police vehicles converging on their location, the males entered their vehicles and tried to drive off. The driver of one vehicle moved back and forth on a couple of occasions, pushing the police vehicles in front of him sufficiently back to allow him to escape. The affected person also drove forward into the police vehicle in front of them, propelling it backwards. They then backed up and accelerated forward again, pushing the police vehicle further back. When the affected person reversed a second time, their vehicle spun to the right and came to a stop; its driver side had struck a light standard. As this was occurring, the subject officer had smashed out the driver’s window with their baton and attempted to incapacitate the affected person through the window with a conducted energy weapon, but neither of the discharges were effective. With the affected person’s vehicle at rest, the subject officer and another officer struggled to remove the affected person from the driver’s seat, at least partly because the driver’s door was wedged against the light standard. The affected person was eventually forced out of the vehicle by the officers and taken to the ground where they were struck several times by the officers before being handcuffed behind the back. Paramedics attended the scene and transported the affected person to hospital where they were diagnosed with a serious injury.

finding: Dossier terminé - Aucune accusation portée par le DPCP

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: In the afternoon of early 2026, the subject officer observed the affected person in the area of a street in Stratford. Aware that the affected person was subject to a warrant for their arrest, the officer contacted uniformed personnel to respond to the area to take the affected person into custody. A witness officer arrived in the area in a fully marked cruiser. The officer pulled up to the affected person as they walked and watched as the affected person reversed directions and ran. The subject officer had stopped their vehicle a short distance away and exited, running across the street and tackling the affected person to the ground. The affected person attempted to right themselves and was held down by the subject officer and the witness officer, who arrived on scene within seconds. There ensued a struggle during which the subject officer delivered several punches to the head and torso of the affected person, and a strike to their side. With the assistance of additional officers arriving on scene, the affected person was handcuffed behind the back. Following their arrest, the affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a fracture.

The MPCC initiated a Public Interest Investigation in early 2021 into the handling by a Military Police Unit of an investigation involving one of its own members who allegedly appeared intoxicated while attempting to drive with children. Complaints alleged that unit leadership covered up the misconduct, altered investigation reports without the investigator's knowledge, improperly directed subordinates not to report the incident, failed to refer the matter to the CFNIS, and failed to report to the Office of Professional Standards. A further related interference complaint was filed regarding an alleged child welfare investigation involving the same officer.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: In the evening, in early 2026, officers from the Ottawa Police Service responded to a reported domestic disturbance in the area of Ottawa. They altered course to a nearby location following a related call about a male on the roof of a building. The subject officer was the first to arrive on scene, followed by two witness officers. The male on the roof was the affected person, who was of unsound mind. They had earlier called police, reporting a concern, and subsequently exited their residence and made their way to the building, ascending onto the roof. The subject officer and a witness officer attempted to persuade the affected person to come down safely. The affected person threatened to jump if not provided with a request and, on a couple of occasions, hung from a structure attached to the top of the exterior wall. As negotiations were ongoing, a fire truck appeared on scene. The affected person eventually lost their grip and fell to the ground, landing feet-first, and was transported to hospital with multiple fractures of both feet.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with police witnesses and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. In the early afternoon of early 2026, the subject officer was dispatched to a gas station following a call to police about a male kicking a door at the premises. The subject officer arrived on scene, parked their cruiser in the parking lot and located the affected person. They detained the affected person and subsequently arrested them on the strength of several outstanding warrants. The subject officer handcuffed the affected person behind the back and conducted a pat-down search by the side of the cruiser, confiscating several items, after which they placed them in the rear of their vehicle. Another officer arrived on scene to take custody of the affected person and transport them to the station. They and the subject officer approached the rear of the subject officer's cruiser, opened the passenger door and observed that the affected person had brought their handcuffed hands to the front and their shoes were off. The officers removed the affected person and subjected them to a secondary search. On this occasion, they located substances in the inner pockets of the affected person's coat. Following the search, en route to the station, the officer observed that something was in the affected person's mouth. They said that they had consumed a substance, at which point the officer pulled over and called for an ambulance. While waiting for the ambulance, the officer pleaded with the affected person to spit out what was in their mouth. Other officers arrived on scene and did the same. The affected person said that they would rather die than go to police cells. Paramedics attended and transported the affected person to hospital. They were unresponsive with persistent bradycardia on arrival. They were monitored overnight and released into the custody of the authorities the following day.

summary_redacted: The Independent Investigations Office (IIO) is responsible for conducting investigations into all officer-related incidents which result in death or "serious harm" within the province of British Columbia. As the Chief Civilian Director of the IIO, they are required to review all investigations upon their conclusion. If they conclude that an officer may have committed an offence, they are required to report the matter to Crown counsel. This is a supplemental public report related to the investigation into the injury of an adult female that occurred in a city in British Columbia. In the first public report, it was indicated that IIO investigators had interviewed civilian witnesses to this incident.
tribunal_body: IIO file 2013-000040 — off duty RCMP officer

Des policiers militaires ont effectué une visite de bien-être auprès d'une ancienne membre de la police militaire dont les enfants avaient été laissés sans surveillance. Des supérieurs de l'unité de police militaire auraient ordonné une « pause tactique » dans l'enquête et contacté la famille de la personne visée pour l'informer de la visite. Un policier militaire ayant refusé d'interrompre l'enquête a fait l'objet d'une enquête disciplinaire d'unité pour insubordination, alléguant des représailles en lien avec sa plainte antérieure. La CPPM a décidé de mener une enquête d'intérêt public sur cette plainte pour ingérence.
tribunal_body: Commission d'examen des plaintes concernant la police militaire du Canada (CPPM)

The incident occurred in Chilliwack, B.C. on November 19, 2007. A hit and run incident happened on Yale Road at Airport Road. The affected person arrived at a rental centre to return a wood chipper. A couple reported the hit and run to the RCMP Detachment. A witness spoke to a store owner about the hit and run driver, and the conversation was overheard by the affected person. The affected person paid for their rental and called their father. The store owner physically prevented the affected person from going upstairs and instructed an employee to call the police. The employee called the RCMP to report a disturbance. The RCMP was called back to confirm if police presence was still needed, and assistance was confirmed. The RCMP dispatched a request for members to attend.
tribunal_body: CRCC file PC-2007-2427 — Appendices: Chair's Interim Report -  Public Interest Investigation into the   In-Custody Death of Robert Thurston Knipstrom

summary_redacted_fr: Une officière de la police militaire a été interceptée à la sortie d'un établissement alors qu'elle semblait en état d'ébriété et tentait de prendre le volant avec ses enfants. Des personnes ont allégué que des supérieurs hiérarchiques du détachement ont modifié sans autorisation le dossier d'enquête, exercé des pressions pour éviter le dépôt d'accusations, et omis d'informer les autorités compétentes. La CPPM a conclu que l'allégation de modification inappropriée du dossier d'enquête était partiellement fondée, tandis que les autres allégations étaient non fondées. finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the morning of early 2026, a constable was a Community Intervention Response Team (CIRT) officer on patrol in their marked cruiser when their Automatic Licence Plate Recognition (ALPR) device detected a stolen vehicle - a vehicle - parked outside a market in Brampton. The officer radioed for the assistance of another officer. The subject officer responded to the request and arrived in the area. Notwithstanding the tinted windows on the vehicle, it was determined that there was a person in the driver’s seat of the vehicle. The constable positioned their cruiser directly behind the vehicle to prevent its escape as the subject officer went to the driver’s door and opened it. Their intention was to arrest the driver. The affected person was sitting in the vehicle and gripped the steering wheel with their hand as the subject officer, joined shortly by the constable, attempted to forcibly extricate them. The subject officer delivered several punches to the affected person’s head and upper body area. The affected person was forced out of the vehicle, taken to the ground and punched again in the abdomen by the subject officer before they were handcuffed behind the back. Following their arrest, the affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with a broken nose.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and other witnesses (police and non-police), and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the afternoon of early 2026, the affected person was at a public square to protest against a demonstration by a certain movement. The demonstrators had made their way to the public square from another location, where they had initially gathered. The affected person, with a group of other people, had also been at the other location attempting to disrupt the demonstration. Police officers had been assigned to police the demonstration and counter-protest. They were to preserve the peace and attempt to keep the rival factions separated. At the public square, officers used their bicycles to erect a perimeter between the demonstrators, at the centre of the square, and the counter-protesters, on its periphery. The affected person was in the area of the northeast corner of the square when they and others were asked to leave the square. The affected person began to slowly walk away but stopped to object to the treatment of a woman, who was being physically escorted by police. The subject officer ordered them to leave or they would be arrested for obstructing police. The officer then pushed the affected person by the arms towards the roadway that bordered the square. The affected person told the officer that they did not have to touch them and turned to face the officer. The officer reacted by turning them around and pushing them in the back towards the roadway. The affected person stumbled forward and fell onto the sidewalk, fracturing their hip in the process. The affected person was transported by paramedics to hospital and treated for their injury.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police witnesses, and video footage that captured the incident in part, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, neither subject officer agreed to an interview with the SIU. They did authorize the release of their notes. In the evening of early 2026, the subject officer was on patrol operating a marked cruiser. Their passenger and partner was another subject officer. At about 9:41 p.m., the officers began to follow a vehicle for failing to stop at a red light before making a right turn onto a street and heading south. The vehicle quickly turned left into an apartment complex and then left again onto a ramp to the underground parking for a building situated in the area. The cruiser, its emergency lights activated while stopped behind the vehicle, followed the vehicle as the entry gate rose and the garage door opened. The affected person was driving the vehicle. They continued down a lane between rows of parking spaces, reversed into a parking spot and exited their vehicle. They were immediately confronted by one of the subject officers asking where they were going. The officers had stopped their cruiser short of the vehicle in the lane. The affected person walked past the officers and towards a wall, against which they began to urinate. They were pinned against the wall by one of the subject officers while they continued to relieve themselves, after which they began to struggle against both officers as they tried to control their arms behind their back. The affected person was told they had failed to stop for police. The parties struggled for about five minutes. With the assistance of additional officers, the affected person was handcuffed and taken into custody. They had been forced to the ground, punched twice in the head by one of the subject officers and drive-stunned multiple times in the back by the other subject officer. The affected person was transported to hospital and diagnosed with rhabdomyolysis, which was attributed to trauma and the use of the CEW.

finding: unsubstantiated
summary_redacted: The Military Police Complaints Commission conducted a Public Interest Investigation into an anonymous complaint alleging that military police members abused detainees at an airfield during a training exercise, and that a commander ignored charges recommended following an investigation. The Final Report was released, with an addendum added later.

summary_redacted_fr: Un décès est survenu lors d'une intervention policière à Parent, en Mauricie. Le corps de police impliqué est la Sûreté du Québec. L'incident s'est produit au début 2024.

summary_redacted: A person died from medical complications after a fall with probable head trauma in a care facility. The circumstances surrounding their death raise questions about the application of fall protocols and respect for care wishes.

summary_redacted_fr: Une jeune fille est décédée par asphyxie causée par une contrainte dans un contexte de mauvais traitements dans la région de Granby. Malgré de multiples rapports et suivi, les interventions sont restées fragmentées et insuffisantes.

Une femme autochtone sans abri est décédée d'une crise cardiaque à l'hôpital de Val-d'Or après avoir été admise pour des symptômes respiratoires et de la faiblesse. Le décès a été attribué à un état de choc d'étiologie indéterminée, dans le contexte d'une cardiomyopathie toxique sévère et d'un trouble d'utilisation de psychostimulants chronique. L'enquête a mis en évidence une offre de services fragmentée pour les personnes ayant des problèmes complexes et des limites dans la continuité des soins, le partage d'informations et la coordination entre les acteurs du réseau de la santé, les ressources communautaires et les services pour les Autochtones.

finding: Enquête fermée par la direction du BEI
summary_redacted_fr: Blessure grave lors d'une intervention policière impliquant un véhicule à La Tuque, en Mauricie, au début 2024. Le corps de police impliqué est la Sûreté du Québec.

summary_redacted_fr: Blessure grave – Détention par un corps de police. Lieu : Nord-du-Québec. Période : début 2026. Corps de police impliqué : Nunavik Police Service.

summary_redacted: An adolescent with autism spectrum disorder died by asphyxiation at their home. The coroner's report raises issues related to the identification of suicidal risk and the coordination of interventions between health, school, and community settings.

A person died from acute pulmonary edema in a residential facility due to exposure to excessive heat. They had pre-existing health conditions. Despite interventions by staff, their condition deteriorated.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: The evidence collected by the SIU, including interviews with the affected person and police and non-police eyewitness, and video footage that captured the incident, gives rise to the following scenario. As was their legal right, the subject officer did not agree to an interview with the SIU or the release of their notes. In the morning of early 2026, the subject officer and another officer were dispatched to a convenience store in a certain area. A store employee had contacted police to report a male and female shoplifter. The officers arrived on scene and spoke with store staff, confirming that two suspects had left the store with alcohol and food without paying for the items. The employee showed a video clip they had taken with their cellphone, depicting the male and female. The officers located the suspects – the affected person and another individual – at a nearby bus shelter. One suspect was taken into custody by one of the officers without incident. The affected person, however, resisted when the subject officer attempted to secure them in handcuffs. The officer was able to affix a cuff to one hand but had difficulty attaching the other cuff to the affected person’s other hand. The affected person objected to their arrest, denied that they had done anything wrong, and struggled against the subject officer’s efforts to bring their arms behind their back. The struggle eventually made its way outside the bus shelter where the other officer intervened. The officers brought the affected person to the ground, after which the other officer secured both their hands in handcuffs. Following their arrest, the affected person was taken to hospital and treated for a fractured wrist.
A motorcyclist died in a collision on a route in a Quebec municipality. The coroner's office recommended adding warning rumble strips to the road.
A person died from a polyintoxication involving methamphetamine, cocaine, and fluorofentanyl in Montreal. The circumstances surrounding the death raise concerns about overdose prevention.

finding: grounds_found
summary_redacted: The Independent Investigations Office (IIO) conducted an investigation into an officer-related incident that resulted in serious harm to an adult female in the city of Vancouver. The affected person, an innocent bystander, sustained a serious injury while an adult male was being taken into custody. The Chief Civilian Director of the IIO reviewed the investigation and determined whether an officer may have committed an offence. The IIO has reported the matter to Crown Counsel or provided a summary of the results of the investigation.

Un homme est décédé d’une insuffisance respiratoire dans un contexte de pneumopathie interstitielle dans une région. Les circonstances entourant le décès soulèvent des préoccupations quant à la surveillance constante et à l’évaluation des risques d’obstruction des voies respiratoires.

summary_redacted: A person died by asphyxiation in a detention cell at a courthouse, prompting concerns about cell security, camera blind spots, and surveillance effectiveness.

A person with multiple neurodevelopmental disorders and significant comorbidities died of bilateral broncho-pneumonia in a long-term care facility. The circumstances surrounding their death raise questions about medical evaluation after a fall, the decision to quickly orient care towards a comfort approach, and the keeping of medical records in the facility.

summary_redacted_fr: Une personne décède à la suite d’un polytraumatisme contondant consécutif à une précipitation volontaire dans le vide, à Montréal. Les circonstances entourant le décès révèlent que la personne souffrait de divers symptômes physiques et mentaux depuis plusieurs années.

summary_redacted: A person died from internal bleeding caused by a collision with a wall in a parking lot. The driver had a documented condition that raised questions about medical evaluation for driving ability.

finding: no_grounds
summary_redacted: In the early morning of February 2026, the subject officer sat stationary in their marked cruiser in a parking lot at the southeast corner of a major intersection. They and their passenger were manning a perimeter point at that location. At about 2:43 a.m., two vehicles travelled past their location at excessive speed. The subject officer pulled onto the roadway and accelerated after the vehicles, intending to stop them in connection with their speed. With the driver were several passengers, including the affected person in the backseat. The vehicles continued at speeds as high as 170 km/h. Approaching a major road, the driver came upon traffic stopped at a red light, applied their brakes but crashed into two of the vehicles. The affected person suffered a serious injury in the collision. The subject officer arrived on scene and arrested the driver for dangerous driving.
